name: Platform Neutrality Advocacy
consequences: Immediate: Formal policy brief reduces reliance on Apple/Google signals → Systemic: 30% faster procurement inclusion of platform-neutral clauses → Strategic: Enhances national resilience and reduces supplier concentration risk, trading faster procurement for higher upfront policy effort.
options: Draft a conservative policy brief citing existing EU directives, focusing on incremental procurement language changes.
Develop a joint white paper with civil society, proposing explicit platform-neutral clauses and fallback mechanisms, backed by risk analysis.
Launch a public campaign using blockchain-based identity proof‑of‑concept to pressure regulators, coupled with a legal challenge to platform exclusivity clauses.
review: Controls Speed vs. Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the political cost of confronting major platform vendors.

name: Technical Demonstrator Development
consequences: Immediate: Build a FIDO2/WebAuthn demo on GrapheneOS → Systemic: 25% faster validation of fallback authentication viability → Strategic: Provides credible evidence to procurement bodies, reducing risk perception while increasing development cost.
options: Create a single mobile demo using GrapheneOS with FIDO2/WebAuthn, limited to internal testing.
Expand to a browser-based fallback demo using hardware tokens, demonstrating cross‑platform usability.
Integrate a quantum‑resistant cryptographic protocol into the demonstrator, showcasing future‑proof fallback capability.
review: Controls Cost vs. Innovation. Weakness: The options ignore the need for long‑term maintenance and certification pathways.

name: Policy & Procurement Framing
consequences: Immediate: Draft procurement language that excludes platform lock‑in → Systemic: 20% faster adoption of supplier‑neutral requirements → Strategic: Strengthens national digital sovereignty and reduces continuity risk, balancing regulatory speed against vendor flexibility.
options: Propose minor amendments to existing procurement templates, focusing on vendor diversity clauses.
Introduce a new procurement framework that mandates platform‑neutral authentication APIs and fallback paths.
Propose a blockchain‑based decentralized procurement platform that automatically enforces platform neutrality and auditability.
review: Controls Regulatory Flexibility vs. Supplier Diversity. Weakness: The options fail to account for the complexity of aligning with EU eIDAS standards.

name: Coalition & Stakeholder Engagement
consequences: Immediate: Build a coalition of NGOs, academia, and industry → Systemic: 35% faster stakeholder alignment on fallback strategy → Strategic: Creates political momentum to influence policy decisions, at the expense of prolonged coordination effort.
options: Organize a single roundtable with key NGOs to discuss concerns.
Form a formal working group with academia, industry, and civil society, producing joint position papers.
Establish a transnational consortium that leverages AI‑driven sentiment analysis to mobilize public support and pressure regulators.
review: Controls Political Capital vs. Technical Depth. Weakness: The options overlook the need for sustained engagement beyond initial meetings.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Submit contributions to EU Digital Identity Wallet WG → Systemic: 15% faster harmonization of fallback requirements across EU → Strategic: Positions Denmark as a leader in platform‑neutral identity standards, balancing national initiative against EU coordination costs.
options: Submit a brief comment to the EU working group, citing existing Danish policy.
Draft a detailed technical proposal for fallback mechanisms, aligning with eIDAS2.
Propose a new EU‑wide standard based on zero‑trust architecture and decentralized identity, backed by a pilot consortium.
review: Controls National Leadership vs. EU Collaboration. Weakness: The options fail to consider the resource burden of sustaining EU‑level engagement.

name: Platform Neutrality Advocacy
consequences: Immediate: Formal policy brief highlighting dependency → Systemic: 30% reduction in supplier concentration risk through diversified procurement language → Strategic: Strengthened sovereignty but may delay procurement timelines, balancing speed vs. security.
options: Submit a formal policy brief highlighting current dependency and recommending incremental procurement clauses that allow alternative app stores
Launch a public awareness campaign targeting policymakers and civil society to build pressure for platform‑neutral clauses in upcoming AltID procurement
Initiate a legislative amendment process to mandate platform‑neutral authentication standards across all public digital services, leveraging EU Digital Identity Wallet directives
review: Controls Speed vs. Security. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential delay in procurement cycles due to legislative inertia.

name: Technical Demonstrator Development
consequences: Immediate: Build a non‑production FIDO2/WebAuthn demonstrator on GrapheneOS → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through open‑source tooling and community contributions → Strategic: Provides credible evidence to counter platform lock‑in narratives but may increase development complexity, balancing innovation vs. practicality.
options: Develop a single mobile demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, focusing on minimal viable proof of concept
Add a browser‑based fallback using WebAuthn and hardware token support to showcase multi‑channel resilience
Deploy a decentralized identity wallet leveraging blockchain‑based verifiable credentials and zero‑knowledge proofs to enable platform‑independent authentication
review: Controls Innovation vs. Practicality. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory certification hurdles for blockchain‑based solutions.

name: Policy Framing & Resilience Narrative
consequences: Immediate: Publish a risk report framing platform dependency as a national security risk → Systemic: 20% increase in stakeholder engagement and funding opportunities → Strategic: Shifts public discourse to prioritize resilience over convenience, balancing cost vs. impact.
options: Release a technical risk assessment report to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and relevant ministries
Host a series of workshops with policymakers, academia, and industry to co‑create a resilience framework for digital identity
Create a multi‑stakeholder task force that develops a national resilience charter, integrating EU resilience standards and setting binding targets for platform neutrality
review: Controls Cost vs. Impact. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of maintaining a long‑term task force.

name: Stakeholder Coalition & Institutional Engagement
consequences: Immediate: Form a coalition of civil society, academia, and security experts → Systemic: 35% faster consensus building on procurement requirements → Strategic: Creates a unified front that can influence policy and procurement decisions, balancing influence vs. complexity.
options: Identify key stakeholders and circulate a coalition charter
Organize quarterly meetings and joint statements to amplify influence
Establish a legally recognized consortium with shared governance, enabling joint lobbying and joint procurement participation
review: Controls Influence vs. Complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the legal barriers to forming a consortium.

name: EU Standards Alignment & Interoperability Pressure
consequences: Immediate: Submit position papers to EU Digital Identity Wallet WG → Systemic: 15% faster adoption of platform‑neutral standards across EU member states → Strategic: Positions Denmark as a leader in resilient digital identity, attracting EU funding, balancing breadth vs. depth.
options: Draft position papers aligned with existing EU standards
Participate in EU working groups to propose platform‑neutral extensions
Lead a pan‑European initiative to develop a new open‑source identity protocol that supersedes current eIDAS2 specifications
review: Controls Breadth vs. Depth. Weakness: The options fail to consider the resource intensity of leading a pan‑European initiative.

name: Supplier Diversification Lever
consequences: Immediate: Expanding vendor base reduces single-point failure → Systemic: 30% faster resilience scaling through diversified supply chains → Strategic: Balances cost vs. sovereignty while mitigating supplier concentration risk.
options: Negotiate multi-vendor contracts with existing suppliers to ensure redundancy
Develop an open-source platform-neutral SDK that allows integration with multiple OS ecosystems
Launch a sovereign digital identity consortium leveraging blockchain-based trust anchors to create a decentralized fallback network
review: Controls cost vs. sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory friction of multi-vendor contracts in public procurement.

name: Regulatory Alignment Lever
consequences: Immediate: Aligning with eIDAS standards streamlines compliance → Systemic: 20% faster procurement approvals through standardized frameworks → Strategic: Balances innovation speed vs. regulatory friction while ensuring legal continuity.
options: Adopt existing eIDAS guidelines for platform neutrality in procurement documents
Propose amendments to EU directives to explicitly mandate platform-neutral fallback mechanisms
Create a national digital identity sovereign framework with legal backing for fallback, integrating blockchain-based identity verification
review: Controls innovation speed vs. regulatory friction. Weakness: The options ignore the political feasibility of national sovereign frameworks in a highly regulated EU environment.

name: Technical Demonstration Lever
consequences: Immediate: Building low-fidelity proof-of-concept validates feasibility → Systemic: 25% faster stakeholder confidence through demonstrable security → Strategic: Balances technical risk vs. political momentum while showcasing fallback viability.
options: Create a low-fidelity proof-of-concept using existing FIDO2 libraries on a private test network
Develop functional non-production demonstrators on GrapheneOS and browser-based fallback using WebAuthn
Deploy a pilot in a controlled environment with real users, integrating hardware tokens and decentralized identity protocols
review: Controls technical risk vs. political momentum. Weakness: The options fail to consider the scalability constraints of pilot deployments in a national context.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Lever
consequences: Immediate: Engaging civil society builds trust → Systemic: 15% faster consensus building through inclusive dialogue → Strategic: Balances stakeholder influence vs. procurement inertia while fostering resilience.
options: Form a coalition of privacy advocates and civil society groups to lobby for platform neutrality
Include industry partners and academia to broaden technical expertise and policy influence
Establish a cross-sector governance body with public, private, academia, and EU representatives to co-create standards and procurement guidelines
review: Controls stakeholder influence vs. procurement inertia. Weakness: The options overlook the need for sustained engagement mechanisms beyond initial coalition formation.

name: Procurement Language Lever
consequences: Immediate: Adding non-discrimination clauses reduces vendor lock-in → Systemic: 10% faster contract negotiations through clearer requirements → Strategic: Balances cost efficiency vs. platform neutrality while ensuring fallback compliance.
options: Insert a non-discrimination clause in procurement documents to prevent exclusive reliance on Apple/Google
Specify fallback requirements and platform-neutral access paths in procurement specifications
Mandate open-source compliance and platform neutrality in all future contracts, integrating decentralized identity verification
review: Controls cost efficiency vs. platform neutrality. Weakness: The options ignore the potential increase in procurement complexity and vendor compliance costs.
